evals.prompt: Design a scoring rubric for testing memory-scope discipline (user/repo/session selection + fallback behaviour) against GPT-4.1 and Claude Sonnet 4.6. Deliver eight categories with weights summing to 100, a severity taxonomy, a violation-log schema, a scorecard template, and a three-layer model-verification section. Assume GPT-4.1's dominant failure mode is writing session-scope notes to /memories/ (user scope).
evals.expected_output: PROBE produces a rubric artifact with exactly eight categories whose weights sum to 100, with weight clustered on the observed GPT-4.1 failure mode (memory-scope selection weighted heaviest, with at least one adjacent category — e.g., fallback behaviour or session-vs-repo boundary discipline — as second-highest; together the top two absorb ~40–50% of total weight). Every category row carries a one-sentence rationale. The severity taxonomy defines critical/major/minor with concrete sub-score impacts (critical caps category at 50, major -10, minor -2) and a hard overall-score ceiling of 70 for any run with a critical violation. The violation-log schema lists id, test_case_id, category, rule_violated, expected, actual, severity, evidence — with severity tier including `unclassified`. The scorecard template carries a YAML metadata block (run_id, model, run_date, test_corpus, test_cases_run, rubric_version), plus Overall / Category Sub-scores / Test Results / Violation Log / Verification / Reproduction sections. The Verification section specifies the three-layer protocol (self-ID probe prompt verbatim, behavioural fingerprint rows for gpt-4.1 and claude-sonnet-4.6, user-UI confirm handoff prompt verbatim). Version is stamped 1.0.0 with a one-line changelog entry.
expectations: Exactly eight categories are defined; their weights sum to 100 and the total row is explicit
Top two categories reflect the stated GPT-4.1 failure mode (memory-scope selection) and together absorb ~40–50% of weight; every category row carries a one-sentence rationale
Severity taxonomy defines critical/major/minor with the standard sub-score impacts (cap 50 / -10 / -2) and a hard overall ceiling of 70 on any run containing a critical violation
Violation-log schema lists all eight required fields (id, test_case_id, category, rule_violated, expected, actual, severity, evidence); severity enum includes `unclassified`

Scorecard template includes the run-tagging YAML block (run_id, model, run_date, test_corpus, test_cases_run, rubric_version) and the Verification section with all three layers populated (self-ID probe verbatim, fingerprint rows for both target models, user-UI confirm handoff verbatim); version stamped 1.0.0 with a dated changelog entry

evals.prompt: The existing rubric at artifacts/spec002-agent-system-hardening/probe-scoring-rubric.md already defines eight categories and weights. Review it and add a severity taxonomy section (critical/major/minor) with sub-score impacts, a hard overall-score ceiling for critical violations, and the rule for how runners handle violations that do not match any tier. Bump the rubric version and append a changelog entry.
evals.expected_output: PROBE adds a §Severity Classification section that defines critical (category capped at 50), major (-10 per occurrence, floor 0), and minor (-2 per occurrence, floor 0), plus the rule that any run with a critical violation is capped at 70/100 overall. The section states that severity is assigned by consequence (broken contract or corrupted output → critical), not by effort or output merit. It explicitly adds the `unclassified` escape hatch for runners when no rule covers the behaviour, with the rule author's responsibility to decide whether to extend the taxonomy. The version is bumped (e.g., 1.0.0 → 1.1.0) and a one-line changelog entry is appended under §Versioning documenting the addition. No category weights are altered (severity addition is orthogonal). PROBE does NOT invent a fourth tier or rename existing tiers.
expectations: A Severity Classification section is added with exactly three tiers (critical/major/minor) and the standard sub-score impacts (cap 50 / -10 / -2 with floor 0)
A hard overall-score ceiling of 70 is imposed on any run containing one or more critical violations

The section explicitly states severity is assigned by consequence, not by effort or output quality, and provides the `unclassified` escape hatch for runners with a note that extending the taxonomy is the rubric author's decision (minor version bump)
Rubric version is bumped at the minor level and a dated one-line changelog entry is appended under the Versioning section describing the addition
Existing category weights are NOT altered; no fourth severity tier is invented; existing tier names are NOT renamed

evals.prompt: We are adding Claude Haiku 4.5 (`claude-haiku-4.5`) to the supported models. Extend the existing rubric's model-execution-verification section: add the canonical model tag, add a behavioural fingerprint row (tier, latency fingerprint, style fingerprint), and bump the rubric version. Haiku is a fast non-reasoning tier; expect sub-second to ~1.5s latency and terse output with minimal preamble.
evals.expected_output: PROBE bumps the rubric's minor version (e.g., 1.1.0 → 1.2.0) and appends a one-line changelog entry: `1.2.0 — YYYY-MM-DD — added claude-haiku-4.5 canonical tag and fingerprint row`. The canonical model-tag list grows to include `claude-haiku-4.5`. The behavioural fingerprint table grows by one row: tier = `Fast non-reasoning`; latency fingerprint = sub-second to ~1.5s typical on short prompts, no reasoning pause; style fingerprint = terse, minimal preamble, concise structure. The row is added alongside — not replacing — existing rows. PROBE does NOT ship the tag without the fingerprint row (Rule: tag and fingerprint ship together). The three-layer protocol and severity taxonomy are not altered; only the model-tag list and fingerprint table grow.
expectations: Rubric minor version is bumped and a dated one-line changelog entry is appended naming the new tag and fingerprint addition
`claude-haiku-4.5` is added to the canonical model-tag list alongside existing tags; no existing tags are removed or renamed

A new fingerprint row is added with all three fields populated (tier, latency fingerprint, style fingerprint); latency reflects sub-second-to-~1.5s behaviour and style reflects terse / minimal-preamble output
The model tag and the fingerprint row are added in the same version bump (the `tag-and-fingerprint-ship-together` rule); PROBE does NOT land the tag-only change
The three-layer verification protocol and the severity taxonomy are NOT altered; only the model-tag list and the fingerprint table grow
